subsystem: GPAC avilib AVI/RIFF demuxer. The function avi_parse_input_file() opens an AVI container, walks its top-level RIFF chunks to locate the 'hdrl' header list, the 'movi' data list, and an optional 'idx1' index, then parses the in-memory header list (stream headers 'strh', format chunks 'strf', OpenDML super-index 'indx' chunks) and finally builds the per-track video/audio frame index tables. It is the primary untrusted-input parser of the avilib demuxer.
provenance: Identified by the '[avilib]' log tags routed through GF_LOG(GF_LOG_*, GF_LOG_CONTAINER, ...), the GPAC allocator wrappers gf_malloc/gf_realloc/gf_free, the gf_ftell/gf_fseek/avi_read I/O layer, the AVI_errno/AVI_ERR_* error codes, and GPAC-specific struct/typedef names (alAVISTREAMHEADER, alBITMAPINFOHEADER, alWAVEFORMATEX, avisuperindex_chunk, avisuperindex_entry, s64/u64/u32 typedefs). This is src/media_tools/avilib.c from GPAC (gpac.io, LGPL). The sample banner 'primevul_c_train_2206_vuln' confirms it is a real-world extract.
trust_boundary: The entire AVI file is attacker-controlled. Bytes enter through avi_read(AVI->fdes, ...) and, crucially, the whole 'hdrl' header list is slurped into the heap buffer hdrl_data (line 60/67) and thereafter parsed with pointer arithmetic. Every chunk size (str2ulong on data+4 / hdrl_data+i+4), every tag FourCC, and every embedded field the parser trusts — nEntriesInUse, wLongsPerEntry, bIndexType, bi_size, cb_size, dwSize, total_frames, nrEntries — is drawn verbatim from the file and is therefore hostile. There is no schema/length validation before these values drive allocations, loop bounds, and memcpy lengths.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point taking an open avi_t handle and a getIndex flag. Locals include a signed int chunk cursor 'i' used to walk the header list, an s64 chunk size 'n', the raw header buffer pointer hdrl_data with a signed int hdrl_len for its length, per-track counter arrays nai[AVI_MAX_TRACKS] and tot[AVI_MAX_TRACKS], a 256-byte scratch buffer 'data' for reading chunk headers, and a lasttag state machine variable that records whether the last 'strh' was video (1) or audio (2). The only precondition checked is a non-NULL AVI. Note that 'i', 'hdrl_len', and 'num_stream' are signed ints while chunk sizes originate as unsigned wire values — a sign-domain mismatch that recurs below.

Reads the 12-byte RIFF preamble into data[] and validates the 'RIFF' magic at offset 0 and 'AVI ' at offset 8. This is the ONLY structural sanity gate before the file is trusted as an AVI; it constrains nothing about chunk sizes. data[256] comfortably holds 12 bytes, so this read is safe.

Top-level RIFF chunk scanner. Each iteration reads an 8-byte chunk header (4-byte FourCC + 4-byte little-endian size) into data[], derives the chunk size n from data+4 via str2ulong, and word-aligns it with PAD_EVEN. The oldpos==newpos check is a progress guard: if the file position did not advance since the last iteration the stream is declared broken and the function bails, which prevents the top loop itself from spinning. n is stored in a signed s64 but comes from an unsigned 32-bit reader, so it is in [0, ~4.29e9].

For a 'LIST' chunk it reads the 4-byte list type and subtracts 4 from n. If the list is 'hdrl', it records hdrl_len = (u32)n, allocates hdrl_data of n bytes, remembers header_offset (file position of the header body), and reads n bytes of the header list into the buffer. Note hdrl_len is a signed int assigned from (u32)n: a header list larger than INT_MAX makes hdrl_len negative, and the read compares an int count against n. For 'movi' it records the data-list start and seeks past it; any other list is skipped. The size n used for both allocation and read is fully attacker-chosen with no upper bound.

Handles the legacy 'idx1' index: n_idx = n/16 entries, allocates n bytes cast to a pointer-to-[16] array, and reads n bytes. The in-code comment admits n need not be a multiple of 16; n_idx (entry count) is derived by integer division, so a short read is tolerated but n_idx may still overstate the usable entries. Any unrecognized top-level chunk is simply seeked past. This loop ends when avi_read returns fewer than 8 bytes (EOF).

After confirming a header list and a movi start were found, the code enters the central header-list parser: a for-loop over the cursor i from 0 to hdrl_len. Each iteration inspects the 4-byte tag at hdrl_data+i; nested 'LIST' tags are skipped by advancing 12 bytes. For everything else it reads a 4-byte sub-chunk size n from hdrl_data+i+4 and pads it even. Critically, i is advanced by attacker-controlled amounts (8 plus n) and NONE of the reads at hdrl_data+i, +4, +8, ... are bounds-checked against hdrl_len before dereference. The loop condition i<hdrl_len is the only guard and i is a signed int.

The 'strh' (stream header) branch. It advances i by 8 to reach the stream-type FourCC, and for a video stream copies the 4-byte compressor tag (hdrl_data+i+4), reads scale/rate/frame-count from fixed offsets +20/+24/+32, and finally memcpy's a full sizeof(alAVISTREAMHEADER) from hdrl_data+i into AVI->video_stream_header. Every one of these reads assumes the header list still has that many bytes past i, which is never verified against hdrl_len — the parser trusts the tag placement to imply the payload size.

The audio 'strh' branch. It assigns aptr=anum, increments anum, and rejects only when anum > AVI_MAX_TRACKS (after the increment, so aptr stays within 0..AVI_MAX_TRACKS-1). It then reads audio fields at hdrl_data+i+32/+44/+24 and memcpy's sizeof(alAVISTREAMHEADER) into stream_headers[aptr]. Note auds_strh_seen is never set (the assignment is commented out), so this branch can run for every audio 'strh', incrementing anum and consuming a track slot each time. As with the video branch, the fixed-offset reads and the struct copy are unbounded relative to hdrl_len.

The video 'strf' (format) branch reads a BITMAPINFOHEADER. It copies sizeof(alBITMAPINFOHEADER) from hdrl_data+i into a stack bih, then allocates a heap block of bi_size bytes (a field read straight out of bih, attacker-controlled) and copies bi_size bytes from hdrl_data+i into it. It also reads width/height/compressor2 at fixed offsets and, if the sub-chunk size n>40, copies (n-40) bytes of 'extradata' from hdrl_data+i+40. Both the bi_size copy and the extradata copy read from the header buffer using lengths that are not constrained to the bytes actually remaining in hdrl_data.

The audio 'strf' branch parses a WAVEFORMATEX. Interestingly this branch DOES clamp the initial copy: wfes is min(hdrl_len-i, sizeof(alWAVEFORMATEX)), so the memcpy into wfe respects the remaining header. It then reads cb_size (the trailing extension length) from the just-copied structure and, if non-zero, reallocs wfe to sizeof+cb_size and reads cb_size extra bytes from the FILE (not from hdrl_data) at header_offset+i+sizeof. cb_size is a u16 (max 65535) and the realloc/read pair trusts it directly. This is the one place in the header walk that bounds its buffer read, which sharpens the contrast with the unchecked branches above.

The OpenDML 'indx' super-index branch. It allocates a fixed avisuperindex_chunk, then parses the super-index header directly out of hdrl_data via a walking pointer 'a': FourCC, dwSize, wLongsPerEntry (u16), bIndexSubType/Type bytes, nEntriesInUse (u32), dwChunkId, and 12 reserved bytes. It then allocates the entry table aIndex with size wLongsPerEntry * nEntriesInUse * sizeof(u32) and loops nEntriesInUse times, reading 16 bytes per entry (qwOffset+dwSize+dwDuration) and advancing 'a'. Both the allocation size and the loop trip count are products/values taken straight from the file. The audio branch at lines 333-397 is byte-for-byte identical against AVI->track[AVI->aptr].audio_superindex.

The tail of the header-list loop. Known-but-ignored tags (JUNK/strn/vprp) and any unknown tag advance i by 8, and then EVERY path adds the padded sub-chunk size n via i += (u32) n. This is where the cursor advances by the attacker-controlled payload size. Because i is a signed int and n can be up to ~4.29e9, the addition can overflow i into negative territory, and because no branch verifies i+n <= hdrl_len, i can also jump far past the buffer end while still failing to terminate cleanly. This single statement is the linchpin of the loop's safety.

When is_opendml was set, the code loads each ix## sub-chunk pointed to by the super-index. For each entry it mallocs dwSize+hdrl_len bytes, seeks to the entry's file offset qwOffset, and reads that many bytes into 'en'. It then reads nrEntries and offset from within 'en', grows the flat video_index by nrEntries (nvi += nrEntries), and in an inner while(k<nvi) loop parses 8 bytes per frame out of 'en'. The chunk buffer holds only dwSize+hdrl_len bytes, but the number of 8-byte reads is driven by nrEntries (read from en+12), which is independent of dwSize — so the inner loop can walk far past the allocation. The audio mirror is at lines 613-673.

The multiple-RIFF reconstruction path (also reached via goto multiple_riff when an OpenDML file yielded zero video frames). It trusts AVI->total_frames — a value read from the 'dmlh' chunk (line 181) — as the count of frames, sizing the video_index allocation as total_frames*sizeof(video_index_entry) and then filling it by rescanning the movi list. The subsequent while loop (lines 715-775) breaks when nvi>=total_frames and reads ##db/##wb chunks; unmatched chunks seek back 4 bytes so the 8-byte window slides forward by 4 each iteration until EOF.

The default (non-OpenDML) path builds the frame index from the legacy 'idx1' table. It first counts video/audio entries by matching each 16-byte idx[i] against the generated tags, allocates video_index/audio_index accordingly, then fills them by reading pos/len from idx[i]+8/+12. The counting and filling loops both iterate AVI->n_idx (n/16 from the idx1 chunk); the reads at idx[i]+8 and +12 stay within the 16-byte entry, so this path is comparatively well-behaved as long as n_idx matches the allocation, which it does since both derive from the same idx1 chunk.

Epilogue: reposition the file at the movi data start and reset the read cursor. Note hdrl_data was freed earlier at line 414 before the index-building section; there is no cleanup path that frees the partially-built superindex/aIndex tables on the error returns scattered through the parser, but the dominant risks here are the memory-safety issues in the parsing paths above rather than the leaks.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: wLongsPerEntry (u16, up to 65535) and nEntriesInUse (u32) are read straight from the file. The allocation size wLongsPerEntry * nEntriesInUse * sizeof(u32) is an unsigned 32-bit multiplication that overflows/wraps for large factors (e.g. nEntriesInUse near 2^28), yielding a tiny buffer. The following loop then writes nEntriesInUse fixed 16-byte avisuperindex_entry records into aIndex[j], a controlled heap overflow. Independently, the loop advances 'a' 16 bytes per entry through hdrl_data with no check against hdrl_len, an out-of-bounds READ of the header buffer. The audio clone at lines 363-382 has the identical flaw. Invariant: nEntriesInUse and wLongsPerEntry must be validated and the product checked for overflow before allocating, and each 16-byte read must be bounded against the header buffer.

The header-list parser never checks that i plus the size it is about to read stays within hdrl_len. A crafted hdrl that places a 'strh' tag near the end of the buffer makes this memcpy read sizeof(alAVISTREAMHEADER) bytes past the end of hdrl_data — an out-of-bounds heap read that also lands in AVI->video_stream_header. The same unbounded pattern drives the scalar reads at hdrl_data+i+20/+24/+32 (video), hdrl_data+i+32/+44/+24 and the stream_headers[aptr] copy (audio, lines 155-163), and the superindex byte-walk. Invariant: before dereferencing hdrl_data+i+off, the parser must verify i+off+field_size <= hdrl_len; the whole loop lacks any such per-access bound.

The loop cursor i is a signed int advanced by i += (u32) n (line 411) where n is an attacker-chosen, PAD_EVEN'd 32-bit size. Two failure modes: (1) integer overflow — adding a value near 2^31..2^32 to i overflows the signed int to a large negative value, after which i<hdrl_len remains true and hdrl_data+i becomes a wild pointer before the buffer (CWE-190 feeding CWE-125/787), and the loop can fail to terminate as designed (CWE-835); (2) even without overflow, i can jump past hdrl_len only to be re-checked next iteration, but the tag reads at hdrl_data+i for that iteration already happened out of bounds. Invariant: n must be validated so that i strictly and safely advances within [0, hdrl_len] every iteration, using an unsigned cursor with an explicit remaining-bytes check before adding n.

nvi is grown by nrEntries (line 565), a value read from en+12 that is unrelated to the size dwSize+hdrl_len actually read into 'en'. The inner loop consumes 8 bytes of 'en' per iteration for k in [old_k, nvi), so a large nrEntries reads far past the chunk buffer (CWE-125). nvi = sum of nrEntries is also multiplied by sizeof(video_index_entry) in the realloc at line 566 with no overflow check (CWE-190), which can undersize video_index and turn the writes here into a heap overflow (CWE-787). The empty-chunk branch does k--; nvi--; if it triggers on the first entry (k==0) it decrements k to -1 then k++ back to 0 while shrinking nvi, muddying the bound further. Invariant: nrEntries must be bounded by the bytes actually present in the chunk (dwSize), and nvi*sizeof must be overflow-checked before the realloc.

bi_size is a 32-bit field copied out of the file into bih. The code allocates exactly bi_size bytes and then copies bi_size bytes from hdrl_data+i into it. If bi_size is larger than the number of bytes remaining in hdrl_data past i, the second memcpy reads out of bounds of the header buffer (CWE-125); the destination is sized to match so it is a source over-read into a heap object. bi_size==0 makes gf_malloc(0) whose non-NULL behavior is allocator-dependent, and there is no NULL/short check tying bi_size to hdrl_len-i. Invariant: the copy length must be min(bi_size, hdrl_len - i) and bi_size must be sanity-bounded.

extradata_size = n-40 where n is the sub-chunk size read from the header (str2ulong at line 112, PAD_EVEN'd). The guard n>40 prevents the unsigned subtraction from wrapping, but nothing checks that hdrl_data actually holds n (or 40+extradata_size) bytes past i, nor that i+40+extradata_size <= hdrl_len. A large n with the tag near the buffer end makes the memcpy read (n-40) bytes out of bounds of hdrl_data into the freshly allocated extradata (CWE-125). Invariant: the source copy must be bounded by the real remaining header bytes, not by the self-described chunk size n.

The audio OpenDML index realloc, unlike its video counterpart (which checks !AVI->video_index and exit(1)), does NOT check the return value. If gf_realloc returns NULL under memory pressure (easily provoked because nai[audtr] += nrEntries is attacker-inflated), the following while loop dereferences audio_index[k] at a NULL base plus offset — a crash/NULL-pointer write (CWE-476). The realloc also loses the old pointer on failure (leak) since it assigns straight back into the field. Same nrEntries-vs-buffer over-read as the video path applies here too. Invariant: check the realloc result before use and bound nai[audtr] by the actual chunk contents.

cb_size (u16) is the WAVEFORMATEX extension length. The realloc target is sizeof(alWAVEFORMATEX)+cb_size and the subsequent avi_read writes cb_size bytes at nwfe+sizeof(alWAVEFORMATEX). This is internally consistent (destination and length agree), so it is not itself an overflow; the residual risk is that cb_size is read from wfe AFTER wfe is reassigned to the realloc'd nwfe (line 239), and the seek offset header_offset+i+sizeof(alWAVEFORMATEX) is trusted. The read return value is unchecked, so a short/failed read leaves the extension bytes uninitialized. Lower severity than the buffer issues above but worth confirming the field is validated (CWE-20).

On allocation failure the parser calls exit(1), terminating the entire host process. Since nvi is attacker-inflatable (sum of file-supplied nrEntries), a crafted AVI can force this allocation to fail and take down the whole application — a denial-of-service that is inappropriate for a library demux path, which should propagate an error instead (CWE-703 / improper error handling). Invariant: library code must not exit() on untrusted-input-driven allocation failure.

summary: This is GPAC avilib's avi_parse_input_file(), a demuxer that parses a fully attacker-controlled AVI file. The whole 'hdrl' header list is copied into a heap buffer and then walked with fixed-offset pointer arithmetic that is NEVER bounded against hdrl_len, and multiple embedded 32-bit counts/lengths (nEntriesInUse, wLongsPerEntry, bi_size, nrEntries, total_frames) drive allocations and loops with no overflow or bounds validation. The result is a cluster of high-severity memory-safety bugs: an integer-overflowing super-index allocation feeding a heap write, out-of-bounds header reads, an unbounded/signed-overflowing parse cursor, and unchecked reallocs — trivially reachable by opening a malicious AVI.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point that parses an untrusted AVI file end-to-end.
Only ever filled by 8- or 12-byte avi_read calls; never overflowed.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: int nvi, nai[AVI_MAX_TRACKS], ioff; / u64 tot[AVI_MAX_TRACKS] (lines 9-10)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Indexed by anum/audtr which the AVI_MAX_TRACKS guard keeps in range; the danger is downstream index-table sizing, not these fixed arrays themselves.
Attacker-sized header buffer whose length (hdrl_len as signed int) governs every subsequent unchecked access.
Signed cursor advanced by attacker size with no per-iteration bound; overflow/non-termination and OOB.
Struct copy from header buffer with no i+size<=hdrl_len check; OOB read.
Same unbounded struct copy as video; covered by the unbounded-header-access finding.
Allocation and copy length taken from an embedded 32-bit field, unbounded vs remaining header.
Copy length derived from self-described chunk size, not real remaining bytes; OOB read.
u16 extension length drives realloc/read; internally consistent but unchecked read return and post-realloc field re-read.
Unsigned multiply overflow undersizes buffer; loop then writes nEntriesInUse entries -> heap overflow.
Identical integer-overflow/OOB to the video super-index; same finding class.
Guard runs after the increment and before track[aptr] use, keeping aptr in 0..AVI_MAX_TRACKS-1.
Allocation and n_idx derive from the same idx1 chunk; per-entry reads at +8/+12 stay within the 16-byte entry.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: OpenDML video: nvi += nrEntries; realloc; while(k<nvi) (lines 565-597)
reviewed_anchors.reason: nrEntries independent of chunk size drives OOB read and nvi*sizeof realloc overflow -> heap write.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: OpenDML audio: audio_index = gf_realloc(...) with no NULL check (line 645)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Unchecked realloc result dereferenced in the following loop -> NULL write.
Attacker-driven allocation failure aborts the whole process (DoS).
total_frames-sized allocation with a nvi>=total_frames loop guard; risk is the same file-controlled-count pattern already captured, lower impact here.
Fixed-width little-endian byte readers/macros; they carry no buffer bound themselves, the hazard is at their call sites already enumerated.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: top while(1) RIFF scanner with oldpos==newpos guard (lines 40-92)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Progress guard returns -1 on non-advancing position, so this outer loop terminates.

findings.title: Integer-overflow in super-index entry-table allocation feeds heap buffer overflow
findings.anchor: gf_malloc(AVI->video_superindex->wLongsPerEntry * AVI->video_superindex->nEntriesInUse * sizeof (u32)) then for(j<nEntriesInUse) writing aIndex[j]
findings.bug_class: Heap buffer overflow via unsigned multiplication overflow (undersized allocation)
findings.invariant: wLongsPerEntry and nEntriesInUse must be range-checked and their product (times sizeof(u32)) checked for 32-bit overflow before allocating, and the allocation must be large enough for nEntriesInUse * sizeof(avisuperindex_entry) records actually written.
findings.failure_mode: nEntriesInUse (u32) and wLongsPerEntry (u16) come straight from the file. With e.g. nEntriesInUse near 2^28 the product wLongsPerEntry*nEntriesInUse*4 wraps in 32-bit arithmetic to a small value, so gf_malloc returns a tiny buffer; the loop then writes nEntriesInUse 16-byte avisuperindex_entry records, overflowing the heap and corrupting adjacent allocations (potential RCE). The audio clone at lines 363-382 is identical.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether sizeof(avisuperindex_entry) equals wLongsPerEntry*4 and whether any caller/macro bounds nEntriesInUse; confirm no overflow guard precedes the gf_malloc.

findings.title: Unbounded fixed-offset reads/copies from the header buffer (no i+size <= hdrl_len check)
findings.anchor: memcpy(&AVI->video_stream_header, hdrl_data + i, sizeof(alAVISTREAMHEADER)) and sibling hdrl_data+i+off reads
findings.invariant: Before every hdrl_data+i+off dereference or memcpy, the parser must verify i+off+field_size <= hdrl_len; a tag's presence must not be taken to imply its payload is present.
findings.failure_mode: The whole for-loop trusts tag placement. An hdrl crafted so a 'strh'/'strf'/'auds' tag sits within the last few bytes of the buffer makes memcpy read sizeof(alAVISTREAMHEADER) (and the +20/+24/+32/+44 scalar reads, and stream_headers[aptr] copy) past the end of hdrl_data, leaking adjacent heap memory into AVI fields or crashing on an unmapped page.
findings.what_to_confirm: That no wrapper validates each sub-chunk length against hdrl_len before these accesses; confirm sizeof(alAVISTREAMHEADER) can exceed hdrl_len-i.

findings.title: Attacker-controlled, signed, unbounded header-list loop cursor (overflow / non-termination)
findings.bug_class: Loop with unchecked, overflowing counter (integer overflow -> infinite loop / OOB)
findings.invariant: The cursor must be unsigned and each iteration must guarantee strict, safe forward progress inside [0, hdrl_len]; n must be validated as <= hdrl_len - i before it is added.
findings.failure_mode: n is a PAD_EVEN'd 32-bit size from the header. Adding (u32)n to the signed int i can overflow i to a large negative value, after which i<hdrl_len stays true and hdrl_data+i is a wild out-of-bounds pointer that the next iteration dereferences; the loop no longer terminates as intended. Even without overflow, i can leap past hdrl_len while the current iteration's tag read already occurred out of bounds. This is the CWE-835/CWE-190 core the sample is built around.
findings.what_to_confirm: That hdrl_len can be large enough for n to overflow i, and that no clamp of n against remaining header bytes exists before line 411.

findings.title: OpenDML ix## parsing: nrEntries drives over-read of chunk buffer and overflowing index realloc
findings.anchor: nvi += nrEntries; gf_realloc(AVI->video_index, nvi * sizeof(video_index_entry)); while (k < nvi) { ... en += 4; ... }
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read + heap overflow (unchecked count vs buffer / size multiply overflow)
findings.invariant: nrEntries must be bounded by the bytes actually read into the chunk (dwSize), and nvi*sizeof(video_index_entry) must be overflow-checked before the realloc; the inner loop must not read past en's allocation.
findings.failure_mode: nrEntries is read from en+12, independent of the dwSize+hdrl_len bytes actually loaded into 'en'. A large nrEntries makes the inner while(k<nvi) loop consume 8 bytes of 'en' per iteration far past the allocation (OOB read), while nvi*sizeof can overflow to undersize the realloc'd video_index and turn the writes into a heap overflow (CWE-787/CWE-190). The audio path (lines 620-666) mirrors this.
findings.what_to_confirm: That nrEntries is not clamped against dwSize before the loop and that nvi*sizeof(video_index_entry) has no overflow guard.

findings.title: BITMAPINFOHEADER copy length taken from embedded bi_size (source over-read)
findings.anchor: gf_malloc(str2ulong(&bih.bi_size)) then memcpy(..., hdrl_data + i, str2ulong(&bih.bi_size))
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds heap read (copy length from untrusted field)
findings.invariant: The copy length must be min(bi_size, hdrl_len - i) and bi_size must be sanity-bounded (and non-zero) before allocating/copying.
findings.failure_mode: bi_size is a 32-bit value pulled from the file. The code allocates bi_size bytes and copies bi_size bytes FROM hdrl_data+i; when bi_size exceeds the header bytes remaining past i, the source read runs off the end of hdrl_data, leaking adjacent heap into bitmap_info_header or faulting. bi_size==0 yields gf_malloc(0) with allocator-defined behavior.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether bi_size is ever validated against hdrl_len-i and whether gf_malloc(0) returns NULL in this build.

findings.title: extradata copy length from self-described chunk size n (source over-read)
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds heap read (length not bounded by real remaining bytes)
findings.invariant: extradata_size must be clamped to the header bytes actually available: i+40+extradata_size <= hdrl_len.
findings.failure_mode: n is the chunk's self-declared size; the n>40 guard only prevents the subtraction from wrapping. If n claims more bytes than hdrl_data holds past i+40, the memcpy reads (n-40) bytes out of bounds of the header buffer into the newly allocated extradata. Attacker controls n directly.
findings.what_to_confirm: That n is not validated against hdrl_len before the extradata copy.

findings.title: Unchecked realloc of audio_index dereferenced in following loop (NULL write)
findings.anchor: AVI->track[audtr].audio_index = gf_realloc(AVI->track[audtr].audio_index, nai[audtr] * sizeof(audio_index_entry)); while (k < nai[audtr]) { ... }
findings.bug_class: NULL-pointer dereference / write on unchecked realloc (also leak of old pointer)
findings.invariant: Assign realloc to a temporary, check for NULL before use, and only then commit; never write through the result without a NULL check.
findings.failure_mode: Unlike the video path, this realloc's return value is never checked. nai[audtr] is inflated by attacker-supplied nrEntries, so the allocation can fail and return NULL; the immediately following while loop then writes audio_index[k] fields at NULL+offset, crashing (or worse). The old buffer pointer is also leaked on failure because the result is stored straight back into the field.
findings.what_to_confirm: That no NULL check exists between the realloc and the write loop for the audio index.

findings.title: Process abort (exit(1)) on attacker-driven allocation failure
findings.bug_class: Improper error handling / denial of service (library calls exit())
findings.invariant: A demuxer library must propagate allocation failure as an error return, never terminate the host process.
findings.failure_mode: nvi is the sum of file-supplied nrEntries values; a crafted AVI can force the video_index realloc to fail, causing exit(1) to abort the entire application that linked avilib — a reliable denial-of-service from merely opening a file.
findings.what_to_confirm: That this exit(1) is on a reachable untrusted path (it is) and that callers cannot recover from it.

audit_checklist: Add a per-access bound check (i + offset + field_size <= hdrl_len) before every hdrl_data+i dereference, memcpy, and struct copy in the header-list loop.
Make the header-list cursor unsigned and validate n <= hdrl_len - i (with overflow-safe arithmetic) before i += n so the loop always makes bounded forward progress.
Overflow-check wLongsPerEntry * nEntriesInUse * sizeof(u32) (and nvi * sizeof(video_index_entry)) before every gf_malloc/gf_realloc, and bound nEntriesInUse/nrEntries against the bytes actually read.
Clamp bi_size and extradata_size copies to the real remaining header bytes, not to the self-described chunk size, before memcpy.
Bound nrEntries in the OpenDML ix## loops by the chunk's dwSize so the inner while loops cannot read past the allocated en buffer.
Check every gf_malloc/gf_realloc return value (assign realloc to a temporary) before dereferencing, especially the audio audio_index realloc at line 645.
Replace exit(1) on allocation failure with an error return so a malicious file cannot abort the host process.

Verify sizeof(alAVISTREAMHEADER)/alBITMAPINFOHEADER/alWAVEFORMATEX vs hdrl_len and confirm the WAVEFORMATEX cb_size read return value is checked and fields are re-read from the correct (post-realloc) pointer.
